symbols: risen Christ ascending — victory over death and sin
angels — heavenly witnesses and ministers of the Resurrection
fallen soldiers — the powers of the world overcome by divine power
white garment on Christ — purity, glorification, and the new creation
ruins in background — the old order passing away with Christ's rising
dramatic sky — cosmic significance of the Resurrection event
red robes of donors — earthly authority humbled before divine glory
ermine-trimmed vestments — civic dignity presented in devotion
description: The composition divides between a supernatural event on the left and three donor figures on the right. Christ rises powerfully upward, his body luminous and partially draped in white cloth, accompanied by angels. Below him, Roman soldiers fall back in fear, their armor and weapons scattered. Ruined arches and a landscape stretch into the background under a dramatic sky. On the right, three men in rich dark red robes trimmed with ermine stand in devotional posture, gazing toward the miraculous scene.
theological_description: This painting captures the climactic moment of salvation history: the bodily Resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead. The risen Christ bursts upward with divine energy, fulfilling his own prophecy (Matthew 20:19) and vindicating all his claims. The terrified soldiers echo Matthew 28:4, where the guards shook and became like dead men at the angel's appearance. The angels attending Christ recall the accounts in all four Gospels of angelic witnesses at the empty tomb. The Resurrection is the foundation of Christian faith (1 Corinthians 15:14-17): without it, Paul writes, faith is in vain. The ruins in the background symbolize the Old Covenant order giving way to the New, and death's dominion crumbling (Romans 6:9). The three Avogadri donors, positioned as witnesses in rich civic attire, represent the faithful of every age who encounter the Risen Lord through faith and sacrament. Their presence bridges the historical event and the devotional present, reflecting the theology that every believer participates in Christ's Resurrection (Romans 6:4-5). The dramatic upward movement of Christ prefigures the Ascension and anticipates his return in glory (Acts 1:11). Psalm 16:10 finds its fulfillment here: God did not let his Holy One see corruption.

scripture_refs.reason: The Resurrection narrative: the angel, the terrified guards, and the empty tomb are directly depicted
Paul's declaration that the Resurrection is the foundation of Christian faith
Baptized into Christ's death, raised with him in newness of life; death no longer has dominion
Christ's prediction of his own resurrection on the third day, fulfilled in the scene depicted
John's account of the discovery of the empty tomb and its significance for belief
The upward movement of the risen Christ anticipates the Ascension and the promise of his return
David's prophecy that God would not let his Holy One see corruption, fulfilled in the Resurrection
